speaker: interviewee
text: Are you taking-- can I turn the chair a little bit? This way? OK, any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is an interview of Daniel Geslewitz in Phoenix, Arizona. This is October 20, 1995, and I'm the interviewer. I'm Nina Dorf. And the language we're using is English. I don't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just correcting the fact that we are not in Phoenix, Arizona. We are in Scottsdale, Arizona. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: I am rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me your name, and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Daniel Geslewitz. D-A-N-I-E-L G-E-S-L-E-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had another name that you used until you came to America. What was that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Gedalia Geclewicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-D-A-L-I-A G-E-C-L-E-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell 'Lodz' for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-D-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on August the 14, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm 71, going to be 72.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we're going to do on this first tape is give you a chance to talk about your childhood. Can you tell us about your family in Lodz, growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can tell you about family. I was the youngest of seven children. My father had his own business. He was in the cabinet-makers-- cabinet-making business. All my brothers worked for my father. Was-- I was going to school, attending public school. I had seven years of public school. That's all what was compulsory. And after the seven years, you were out, and you-- that was the end of it. But I used to attend technical school, evening, because as a-- if you learned a trade, that the law required that you work so many hours, and then you-- after noon you have to attend technical school. And this was till the war broke out. There was no more-- no more education for Jewish kids, any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in technical school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, at that time, I was. The war broke out, it was vacation time. But, once this-- after vacation, they-- I went to school, and there was an assembly. And the principal, with a friend of mine, made me get up and leave the-- leave the assembly, because he said there no more education for Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to before the war broke out, though, because this is a good time for us to hear about what it was like growing up in Lodz, besides school. What was it like at your home, in terms of a Jewish home?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as a Jewish home, we were strictly traditional religious. We had a Jewish kashrut, was strictly kashrut. We observed all the Jewish holidays, all the Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell me a little bit about some of the holidays that were important?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yom Kippur. First, Saturday, the most-- best day was Friday night, where the whole family used to gather around the table, and there was a festive meal, and-- and the candles, and the kiddush used to make the wine. And was-- the light was bright and was very, very heartwarming, where looking back, it sort of was-- every Friday was a gathering of the family. And no business, no nothing, and it was-- and left good memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, food that we could not eat the whole week, but on Friday we had food like chicken soup, and fish, and all kind of stuff that usually you did not get during the weekday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your part in preparing for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: My part? My part was going with my mother to the market and help her carry the-- the basket with food. We didn't have no refrigeration, so everything had to be bought fresh. So I helped to carry the basket, whatever she would-- chicken, you know, live chicken and live fish. And then she had to go to the guy-- to the shokhet, and-- and the chicken had to be killed, you know, according to the-- it was prescribed by Jewish law, and then was the fish, and you prepared-- she worked the whole day. Just prepare the Friday and Shabbat meal, because Shabbat-- we didn't cook on Sabbath. So she had-- she had some job on her hand to feed nine people.

speaker: interviewer
text: For two days?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two days. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about some other holidays that were special for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yom-- Yom Kippur was a special, a solemn day. It was kind of hard on a kid, you know, because we couldn't eat. When I grew up, you had to fast, and you couldn't-- you-- you had a hard time, you know, waiting until you got to eat again. And-- but and then after the-- the services, the whole family got together. We broke fast together. And, you know, that was a solemn day. Passover was a good-- Passover was a very welcome holiday, because we got new clothes. We got new shoes, and we got whatever new stuff, and-- which we didn't get often. New-- new suit, new shoe, new shirt, and you're dressed up, you look like a bright new nickel. And so we were-- so Passover was kind of a welcome holiday, because your-- the expectation to get material things, like clothes, and all sorts of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a part in preparing your house for Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: We prepared it-- pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: My part was to do anything my mother asked me. There was-- there was a lot to do. There was go to fetch the matzohs, and-- and keep them separate. And there was a whole operation, Passover, because everything had to be clean. And everything was turned upside down, you know, and-- and prepare food, different food, and-- but somehow, you know, it worked out. Everything, you know, hard work, but when Passover came, and the-- the Seder came, everything was festive and-- and, you know, relaxed. So it was a memorable day for me as a youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What I wanted to know was, you said you-- you went to pick up the matzoh. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know you didn't go to the supermarket. Where--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. The matzoh was-- bakeries, and they were baking matzoh. So you had to go to the bakery and-- and take-- it was not packed like here, you go and get the box of matzoh. So you took a cover-- I remember from-- from pillow cover, because it's clean, had to be clean. And they used to fill it up, and I used to carry it home on my back, because we didn't have any cars, or something like that. So me being the young-- the youngest, and the others had to work, so that was my job, you know, to bring the matzoh home. That was one of my duties. And help out, and whatever I was asked to do, I had to do. What do you remember from the Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Seder was-- the whole family got together, again, and was festive. And it was like renewal. Everything was new clothes, everybody dressed up in their best garments. And-- and-- and the candlelight, the candles lit, and the-- and everybody sitting around, like they sit around the table making kiddush. And-- and-- and it gave you a warm feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there, beside your brothers and sisters and parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was only family.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only family was at the Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no grandparents by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There were no grandparents. My grandparents-- my father came to Lodz from a small town, from a little village in-- in [? Koretz, ?] [? Alishnegetz, ?] I guess it was, or something like that. I don't know exactly. But there was no grandparents. Only was immediate family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there other holidays that you remember as being--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember Shavuos. I remember all the holidays. Sukkos and Shavuos was a nice holiday, because the weather was nice, and you were not so restricted. And there was Sukkos. You-- you-- you have to build a sukkah, and that was my job, helping build a sukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you build it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back-- in the front yard. In front of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- did you live in a house in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it a building?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a building. It was a building, and it was like communal sukkah, for-- for-- for more Jewish families, you know, the-- only mostly the-- the parents used to go and eat in the sukkah, the children didn't go. You know, the brothers. And then, other families, the head of the families used to go and use the sukkah for the same purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your whole building--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --had one sukkah.

speaker: interviewee
text: One or two, it depends. I don't remember. But one, I know for sure. I know it was a bigger one, because it served for the whole building, for all the Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to shul with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he go every week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every-- every-- every Saturday. Never-- no fail. And no fail, every Saturday, he went to shul, and Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear tzitzit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he-- [? European. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wore-- he wore-- he-- his-- he dressed like very nontraditional, I would say. He wasn't-- he didn't have no beard. He-- I remember he had a mustache, yes, but no beard. You couldn't distinguish him between-- but he was-- we were religious and observant. As far as the Jewish-- what was required, but you were preparing food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Lodz, where you lived, was that a Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was-- Lodz as a whole, or-- or just neighborhood?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, tell me a little bit about Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz, I'll tell you a little bit about Lodz. Lodz had about 600,000 people, and it was about half was Jewish. Half of the 600,000 were Jews. And where we lived were mixed, mostly Jewish people, in our building. There was some-- a German family. The-- the landlord was German. He lived in the same building. And there were other Polish families, but the majority were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now go back and tell me a little bit about school, because if you lived in a Jewish community, then was the school mostly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The we-- we lived was a mix, but I attended a strictly Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- was Jewish. And it was always a problem, because the animosity between the Polish people. And they always used to-- walking home from school, they used to beat us up, or you know, or try to find-- and hit us, and so forth. It was always a problem to-- to watch out for yourself, not to get into the wrong place, you know? Because there always a danger that you will get hurt. They-- they didn't kill you, but they beat you up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by living in the city, you knew what street to go to school and the time-- the time-- and they let out the schools at a certain time. They let us out at a different time so we should not, you know, meet or something. You should not-- you should separate them. We always tried to separate ourselves from-- from-- from the Polish kids. You mean that the different schools let the children out at different times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I was in school, we-- we let us out at the different time, because if they-- sometimes same time, there was always a danger of them ganging up and beating up the Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your school-- even though it was a government school-- was all Jewish kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did they use in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home. We strictly-- home, we spoke Yiddish. And between our friends, we spoke Yiddish too. Yiddish was the main language, I would say. And Polish, too, but we used Yiddish a lot. Everybody spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the street, yeah. Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: With friends, relatives, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the marketplace?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the marketplace. At the workplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so your dad, in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the store, your dad spoke Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in the store, in the shop, in our work? Yeah, we spoke Yiddish among ourselves. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your dad's shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a woodworking shop. We used to build kitchen cabinets mostly, and-- and all my brothers worked for them. And then he had some other additional, you know, people, painters and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that mean your brothers, who worked for him, also went to technical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before me, yeah. They all-- yeah, they all went to school. I don't know exactly, but all went to school. And then in order to become a journeyman, you had to have so many years technical school. So, but I was the youngest, but they were already-- they were journeymen. They were craftsmen, which I was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of your brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were cabinetmakers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of them were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five. Together, with me, five.

speaker: interviewer
text: With you, five. So the other four brothers had all been trained as craftsmen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, cabinetmakers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the girls. One was two years older, so she-- she was young, and-- but Chaya-- Helen-- she was a seamstress. She was trained to be a seamstress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of them married before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- only my-- before the war, my sister got married, the older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen got married. And she had a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yosel. And she had a boy, I guess at the beginning of the war, or something like this. He-- he-- the beginning of the war. Right at the beginning, or the start of the war. In this period, I don't know exactly. She had a boy, that was the only nephew I had. Or grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went in to the ghetto, she already had a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a child. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she continued to live, after she was married, in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She-- we all-- we all lived in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see. I forgot what I was going to ask you. I asked you if they were craftsmen for a reason and now I've forgotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- because-- you see, there was no-- very hard to get higher education for Jewish people in Poland. The schools-- the government schools-- they're limited, the amount of Jews. Very-- very limited. It was impossible to get into. So when a child-- we finished public school, which was seven years, you had to learn a trade. So you had lots of craftsmen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- you had to go out in the world and learn so you would be able to sustain yourself. That's why you had-- you had lots of craftsmen, and very few people, none of my friends, went to-- to high school. Because it was practically impossible. But you did go to Jewish school, you said. You want to cheder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to cheder I went, which to learn about praying and, you know, learn the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you learned at cheder and what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned how to-- it was one big room. It was mostly one-- all the Jewish men with the beard, traditional. We had lots of kids with the lots of noise, you know, and this, and he did the best he could to-- to teach us the Jewish alphabet and how to-- you know, to read from the siddur, from the book. And the usual thing. Praying, mostly praying. But not-- not-- other-- they didn't educate us in this school, like mathematics, or-- or, you know, English, Polish, was strictly religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a good student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fair. Never had any-- no complaints. I [? faired ?] better. Otherwise, you know, it wasn't-- it was different. That time you had to be very, you know, respectful of the teacher, and had to do the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's what you did, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I guess I was a pretty good student, but I-- I couldn't further my education, due-- due to the circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had bar mitzvah, but there was no big fuss about it. I went to shul, and they-- and they called me to the torah, and they gave me aliyah. And after this, they had a drink, you know, and some herring and a piece of cake. That was the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't-- you just had an aliyah. You didn't read from the Torah, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I read-- I read part from the Torah. Yeah, I read a part from the Torah and, you know, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No big party, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no party whatsoever. There was-- there was-- there was different. There was-- that I think they had other problems. They were-- and mostly they didn't have the means.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was going to ask you about that. If you thought that everybody had these kinds of simple bar mitzvahs, or was it because your family didn't have the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't-- it was mostly religious. The bar mitzvah was where the boy came of age, was called to the Torah, and it was the bar mitzvah. This-- this was it. There was no party given, because this was not part of the ritual, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What else do you think you want to tell us about your life as a child before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was kind of-- I-- I had a happy childhood, regardless of all the other, you know, things that we faced, being-- as a Jewish child. It was a close-knit family, and we-- I didn't experience any, you know, like, I am being deprived of anything. We-- was enough food in the house, and we lived in two big rooms. I-- and the winter, you know, we didn't know anything else. So I think I had a happy childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't talk about your mom. Would you like to talk about your mom a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. My mom was a very nice person. She-- she worked hard. She never complained. And-- and she had to prepare food, she had a full-time job, just to feed nine people every day. You had breakfast and lunch and-- and, you know, and supper, and then for the holidays-- but she-- she never complained, because she had the means, she had-- we had the money to buy the food, compared to other people that problem-- had problems feeding themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this regard, we were-- we were good off. You know, because we didn't experience any hunger, or we didn't get all the food that we wanted to eat during the week, but there was no hunger. And there was enough food to eat. There was enough bread and butter and herring. There was no, let's say, cold cuts, or other stuff, you know, but basically, there was always-- there-- there was no problem with food. And we were kind of always busy, the family, my father had to shop in the same building, and I could always go and do something, and I was never-- I was never bored. So they used to send you down after school to work in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was voluntary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- I was not-- I was not forced to. But after school, I was kind of-- I felt, oh, well, brothers are working, and I-- and I was glad they let me do something, because they didn't trust me doing more. They let me do menial things, like sweep the floor and those things. But otherwise, you know, they didn't make me work. Till-- till I finished school, then I started to learn the trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a big apartment building with your dad's shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was-- it was four stories high, four story. And we had-- we lived on the-- I would say on the third floor. And the first floor, my father had the shop. And the second floor was a synagogue, a little synagogue. And-- and the-- and the top floor, there were, I guess, two families lived. They were poor people, mostly. They worked in the same room, and they lived in the same room, and everything was done in one room. So compared to them, we had separate, so we were better off.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Is there anything else you want to say about the times before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The time about-- about-- before the war, if I look back, mainly I think about the family and the neighbors and the Jewish life. And-- and-- and I think a lot about it. And it's [INAUDIBLE] I live it. And I remember neighbors, in all those years. I remember name. Everything has gone out, a world disappeared. When I when back to Poland, to, you know, take my daughter and show her where we lived, it was a very sad experience, you know, to look at the building. And from all the people that lived there in that building, and there were children, and nobody was left for me to say hello. So a whole world disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? It just disappeared. And it-- and this makes me very, very sad. You know? And I cannot stop thinking about it. But otherwise in Poland, you know, where we lived, we were quite, you know, content, and-- because of the family life, and so forth. We had each other. You said that the kids always had trouble getting along with each other in school, or after school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the kids. The kids-- if it come to Polish kids, and the Jewish kids--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --there was anti-semitism. So there was-- they were aggressive. They used to beat us up. But between the Jewish kids, there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I was-- I was interested in between the Jewish kids and the Polish kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would-- we never-- we didn't mix. We-- we-- as the Jewish kids, we tried to stay away from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't have any Polish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- not-- not really friends. But we-- we had in the same-- in the same house, the families, the Polish kids lived there, but we had no problems with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- when did you start thinking-- I mean, it sounds like anti-semitism was always--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always there.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a part of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always there. You lived it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when did it get-- when did it start getting frightening? When did you start knowing--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- was getting--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that something was even more wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: --as the German-- as Hitler got more popular, more powerful, the Pollacks imitated him, you know, with the laws, and you could feel it. In the press was there-- the press was more [? fashioned ?] after the after German hate paper. And-- and-- in the stores they put up-- Polish stores-- Christian enterprise, you know what I mean? So the Polish shouldn't walk in the Jewish store. You could feel it everywhere, you know, the hate, you know, against the Jewish people, that we weren't liked, would be putting it mildly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had papers, and books, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you read-- you read the newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read. Yeah, sure. We knew what was going on in Germany. We knew about the concentration camps, and so forth, and all the-- the and the-- and the hate and the propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when do you think you really knew that things were getting dangerous?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- they were always dangerous, but only getting worse. In 1936, they had the pogrom in Lodz, not a big one, but they used to, you know, to go into Jewish homes and-- and destroy everything. And you could feel it, that the press, the-- the-- you know, the government turned more anti-semitic. You know, you could feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever talk with your family about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't talk about leaving. We just talked about the situation, that there was-- we had no means to leave. And we lived for so many hundreds of years. And I was a youngster, I-- especially me, I didn't think about leaving. If they talked about it I don't know, but we didn't have the means of-- of leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents say about the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were always afraid, always talking about it's getting bad, and it's getting worse every-- every day. But we were trapped. You know? He didn't have a place where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was really no way to consider leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was no place to leaving. We didn't have the means, and we have no-- we didn't have a place where to. And that was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna stop now, and then we're gonna go on to another tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: What time is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's almost 11:00.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you going to continue for another half hour, then we have lunch?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we're gonna turn off this tape first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--

speaker: interviewer
